text: The surgeon uses a monopolar scissor in the right hand and a PK dissector to prepare the regius cavity and ventral aspect of the prostate during robot-assisted laparoscopic radical prostatectomy. Bipolar coagulation is applied with the left hand, while a Prograsp instrument is used for retraction.

The surgeon begins preparation in the midline near the umbilicus by incising the peritoneum and releasing the bladder from the ventral abdominal wall. The dissection continues towards the pubic bone and symphysis, using a monopolar scissor and PK dissector to pull the bladder down, revealing the pubic bone, symphysis, and ventral aspect of the prostate. The robotic instruments create an extraperitoneal space similar to a balloon trocar technique.

The surgeon identifies the junction between the ligamenta umbilicale medial and the vas deferens at the crossing above the iliac artery. Using a monopolar scissor and PK dissector, the iliac vein, obturator nerve, and endopelvic fascia are exposed. The remaining tissue between the lateral and dorsal aspects of the prostate is bluntly dissected, and the procedure is repeated on the contralateral side.

This technique has been performed in more than five hundred cases, it lasts about four to five minutes, no complications have been observed, it provides maximal exposure of the surgical area, in cases of lymph node dissection it prepares the area and mostly it's simple and easy to be learned from an inexperienced consult surgeon.
